title: SEGMENT 10 — Defining "embryo": the question that won't go away

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Defining "embryo": the question that won't go away
Welcome back.
We spent the first half of the lecture mostly on what synthetic embryos are and how they're made, with one detour into the policy history.
The second half is going to be more conceptual and forward-looking.
We're going to start with what may be the deepest question in the field: what counts as an embryo?
This sounds like a definitional quibble, but it isn't.
It's actually the question that drives most of the ethical debate, most of the policy work, and a fair amount of the scientific debate too.

If we don't know what an embryo is — or rather, if we don't agree on what an embryo is — then we can't say which structures are subject to embryo-research rules, which are not, which require which kinds of oversight, which can be made freely and which need authorization.
The definition is doing real work.
For most of history, the definition of "embryo" was simple in practice if complex in philosophy.
An embryo was what you got when an egg was fertilized by a sperm.
The product of conception.
The early developmental stages of an organism, before the fetal period.
There were edge cases — twinning, molar pregnancies, parthenogenesis in some species — but the central concept was clear.
Egg plus sperm, divisions begin, embryo exists.
Synthetic embryos blow this up.
No egg.
No sperm.
No fertilization event.

But you still get a structure with trophoblast on the outside and pluripotent cells on the inside, with the right cavity, with the molecular markers of an early embryo, capable of triggering implantation responses in maternal tissue.
Is that an embryo?
The field has roughly three positions on this question, and I want to walk you through them so you can have your own opinion.
Position one: an embryo is defined by its origin.
On this view, an embryo is what you get when egg and sperm combine and start developing.
Synthetic embryos, no matter how realistic, are not embryos in this strict sense, because they don't come from a fertilization event.
They're models of embryos.
They may be very good models, but they're still models.
They should be regulated as research tools, with appropriate oversight, but not as embryos per se.
The ethical concerns about destroying embryos don't apply, because you're not destroying anything that was ever an embryo.
This position has the virtue of clarity.
It draws a bright line, and it's hard to argue with on definitional grounds — if the word "embryo" means "the product of fertilization," then synthetic embryos aren't embryos.
But it has the weakness that it can lead to counterintuitive conclusions.

If a synthetic embryo is morphologically and functionally indistinguishable from a real embryo, can really implant in a uterus, can really develop into a fetus given the right environment — does it really make moral sense to treat it as a totally different kind of object, just because its origin is different?
Position two: an embryo is defined by its developmental potential.
On this view, an embryo is anything that, given the right conditions, can develop into a full organism.
This focuses not on origin but on what the structure is capable of becoming.
By this definition, a real embryo is an embryo because it can become a baby; a synthetic embryo is an embryo if and only if it can also become a baby.

Under this view, the question of whether a particular synthetic structure is an embryo depends on its developmental potential, which depends on the structure's completeness, fidelity, and capacity to implant and continue developing.
This position aligns well with traditional ethical reasoning about embryos — the special status of embryos has typically been linked to their potential to develop into people.
If synthetic embryos really have that potential, they share that special status.
If they don't, they don't.
The weakness is that "potential" is hard to measure.
Many synthetic embryos can't develop very far.
Some real embryos can't develop very far either — they have genetic defects that doom them.
Where do you draw the line?
Position three: an embryo is defined by morphological and molecular similarity.
On this view, what matters isn't origin or potential but resemblance.
If a structure looks like an embryo and expresses the same molecular markers, then it should be treated like an embryo, regardless of how it was made.

This is essentially the position the 2021 ISSCR guidelines move toward — they call the most embryo-like synthetic structures "integrated embryo models" and recommend they be treated comparably to real embryos.
The strength of this position is that it's pragmatic.
You can examine a structure, look at its morphology, sequence its cells, decide how embryo-like it is, and apply rules accordingly.
The weakness is that "similar enough" is itself a judgment, and the line is fuzzy.
Synthetic embryos vary in how well they recapitulate real embryos.
Where do you set the threshold?
In practice, the field uses a mix of these definitions.

Regulatory bodies tend to use a combination of origin and similarity — a synthetic structure is treated more like an embryo the more closely it resembles one and the more developmentally complete it is.

Scientific descriptions of these structures often use careful language — "blastocyst-like structure," "embryo model," "stem-cell-derived embryo-like structure" — to avoid implicitly committing to any one definition.
But the underlying disagreement is real, and it surfaces in different ways.
It surfaces when journalists ask whether scientists have "created human embryos in the lab" — the answer depends on which definition you use.
It surfaces when ethicists debate whether destroying a synthetic embryo is morally problematic — depends on the definition.
It surfaces when regulators decide whether a synthetic-embryo experiment needs IVF-clinic-grade oversight or just regular biological research review — depends on the definition.
My own view, for what it's worth, is that the definition question doesn't have a clean answer because the underlying ethical concept is doing too many jobs at once.
The concept of "embryo" was developed in a world where the only embryos were the products of conception, and where their moral status was tied to their potential to become babies.
In that world, the definition didn't need to be precise — every embryo was both originated by fertilization and possessed of developmental potential, so you didn't have to choose.
Synthetic embryos pull these criteria apart.
Now you can have something with embryonic potential but non-embryonic origin, or with embryonic origin but no real developmental potential.
The old concept doesn't slice cleanly through these new objects.
The honest answer, I think, is that "embryo" is becoming a family of related concepts rather than a single concept, and that ethical and legal frameworks will have to track that pluralization.

Different kinds of embryo-like objects will need different rules, and the rules will depend on what features the object has — origin, completeness, developmental progress, capacity to implant — rather than on a binary "embryo or not.
The field is working this out, slowly, in real time.
There's no clean answer yet.
And the next two segments — about the most recent human synthetic-embryo work — are going to make the question even more urgent.
